 {"id":"process_triage-127t","title":"Implement goal-oriented optimization tests","description":"## Test Requirements: Goal-Oriented Optimization (Section 11.10)

### Unit Tests
1. **Expected loss computation**: Test EL = p(abandoned) × cost_false_negative + p(useful) × cost_false_positive
2. **Pareto frontier computation**: Test finding non-dominated kill sets
3. **Constraint satisfaction**: Test memory/CPU budget constraints
4. **Priority ordering**: Test lexicographic ordering by expected gain

### Optimization Algorithm Tests
1. **Greedy selection**: Test greedy kill set selection
2. **ILP formulation**: Test integer linear programming formulation
3. **Constraint propagation**: Test constraint propagation for infeasible states
4. **Local search**: Test local search improvements to greedy solution

### Test Scenarios
```
SCENARIO: memory_pressure
  Goal: Free 10GB memory
  Processes: [A: 5GB, p_abandon=0.9], [B: 3GB, p_abandon=0.7], [C: 8GB, p_abandon=0.3]
  Expected: Select A+B (8GB, lower risk) over C (8GB, higher risk)

SCENARIO: minimize_kills
  Goal: Free 5GB with minimum kills
  Processes: [A: 2GB, p=0.9], [B: 2GB, p=0.9], [C: 5GB, p=0.8]
  Expected: Select C (1 kill) over A+B (2 kills)

SCENARIO: confidence_threshold
  Goal: Kill only if confidence > 0.95
  Processes: [A: p=0.99], [B: p=0.90], [C: p=0.80]
  Expected: Select only A

SCENARIO: value_maximization
  Goal: Maximize expected value recovered
  Processes: [A: 10GB, p=0.5], [B: 2GB, p=0.99]
  Expected: Select B (EV=1.98GB) over A (EV=5GB) if risk-averse
```

### Integration Tests
1. **Multi-objective optimization**: Test Pareto frontier with 3+ objectives
2. **Dynamic re-optimization**: Test updating kill set as processes change
3. **User preference learning**: Test learning user risk preferences from decisions

### Logging Requirements
- Log objective function evaluations
- Log constraint violations
- Log Pareto frontier points
- Log optimization convergence

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-15o4","title":"Implement renewal reward theory for resource estimation","description":"## Section 4.20 - Renewal Reward Theory

**Purpose**: Estimate expected resource recovery from killing processes. Each kill is a 'renewal' with associated reward (freed memory, CPU). Long-run average = E[reward]/E[cycle time].

**Mathematical Background**:
- Renewal process: N(t) = max{n: S_n ≤ t} where S_n = Σ_{i=1}^n X_i (inter-renewal times)
- Renewal reward theorem: lim_{t→∞} R(t)/t = E[R]/E[X] a.s.
- Reward process: R(t) = Σ_{i=1}^{N(t)} r_i where r_i is reward at renewal i
- Age and residual life: A(t) = t - S_{N(t)}, B(t) = S_{N(t)+1} - t
- Key renewal theorem: lim_{t→∞} P(B(t) > x) = (1/μ) ∫_x^∞ P(X > u) du

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `estimate_renewal_rate(kill_times)` - Fit inter-arrival distribution
2. `expected_reward(kill_rewards, renewal_distribution)` - E[R]/E[X]
3. `resource_recovery_forecast(kills_planned, time_horizon)` - Project savings
4. `age_distribution(processes)` - Current age distribution for residual life

**Why This Matters for pt**:
Goal-oriented mode asks 'how many kills to free 10GB?'. Renewal theory gives the answer: if avg memory per zombie = 500MB, expect ~20 kills. Plus confidence intervals.

**Integration Points**:
- Goal-oriented optimization (Section 5.5)
- Resource recovery estimation (Section 6.2)
- Fleet resource planning (Section 3.8)

**Test Requirements**:
- Verify renewal reward theorem holds in simulation
- Verify forecasts match empirical outcomes
- Test with heavy-tailed renewal distributions (realistic for processes)","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead.  {"id":"process_triage-167","title":"Implement maximal tool installation system (Phase 3a)","description":"## Task
Implement the bash wrapper's tool installation system that attempts to install all available instrumentation tools.

## Background
Section 3a specifies 'always try to install everything':
- Detect OS and package manager
- Attempt full install; continue on individual failures
- Use non-interactive installs
- Record what's available vs missing
- Download pinned binaries when packages unavailable

## Package Lists by Platform
**Debian/Ubuntu (apt)**:
sysstat, linux-tools-common, linux-tools-$(uname -r), bpftrace, bcc, bpftool, iotop, nethogs, iftop, lsof, atop, sysdig, smem, numactl, turbostat, powertop, strace, ltrace, ethtool, iproute2, conntrack-tools, cgroup-tools, acct, auditd, pcp, gdb, elfutils, binutils

**Fedora/RHEL (dnf)**: Similar set with dnf package names

**Arch (pacman)**: Similar set with pacman package names

**Alpine (apk)**: Reduced set for minimal environments

**macOS (Homebrew)**: lsof, htop, plus native tools (fs_usage, sample, spindump, powermetrics)

## Implementation Notes
- Script in bash for wrapper
- Use sudo -n (non-interactive) to avoid hanging
- Log all install attempts
- Create capabilities manifest after install
- Handle daemonized packages carefully (don't auto-start auditd)

## Deliverables
- Bash script: pt-install-tools
- Per-platform package lists
- Capabilities manifest generator
- Installation log format
- Documentation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

-{"id":"process_triage-18z","title":"Add TTY detection and NO_COLOR support","description":"## Purpose
Add proper terminal detection to ensure colors/styling only appear when appropriate, following accessibility standards.

## Parent Epic
Console Output Styling Enhancement (process_triage-y8e)

## Current State
The pt script uses colors unconditionally via gum. No fallback exists when:
- Output is piped (`pt scan | grep something`)
- Running in CI environment
- User has NO_COLOR set (accessibility)
- Terminal doesn't support colors

## Implementation

### 1. Add Detection Variables (near top of script)
```bash
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Terminal detection
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Detect if we're in an interactive terminal
IS_TTY=false
[[ -t 1 ]] && IS_TTY=true

# Detect if colors should be disabled
# NO_COLOR is a standard: https://no-color.org/
USE_COLOR=true
if [[ "$IS_TTY" \!= "true" ]] || [[ -n "${NO_COLOR:-}" ]]; then
    USE_COLOR=false
fi

# Detect CI environment
IS_CI=false
[[ -n "${CI:-}" ]] && IS_CI=true
```

### 2. Conditional Color Definitions
```bash
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# ANSI color codes (disabled if NO_COLOR or non-TTY)
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------

if [[ "$USE_COLOR" == "true" ]]; then
    RED='\033[0;31m'
    GREEN='\033[0;32m'
    YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
    BLUE='\033[0;34m'
    CYAN='\033[0;36m'
    MAGENTA='\033[0;35m'
    BOLD='\033[1m'
    DIM='\033[2m'
    RESET='\033[0m'
else
    RED='' GREEN='' YELLOW='' BLUE='' CYAN='' MAGENTA=''
    BOLD='' DIM='' RESET=''
fi
```

### 3. Update Gum Availability Check
```bash
# Gum should only be used in interactive TTY, not in CI
GUM_AVAILABLE=false
if [[ "$IS_TTY" == "true" ]] && [[ "$IS_CI" \!= "true" ]] && command -v gum &>/dev/null; then
    GUM_AVAILABLE=true
fi
```

## Testing
```bash
# Should show colors
pt scan

# Should NOT show colors
pt scan | cat
NO_COLOR=1 pt scan
CI=true pt scan
```

## NO_COLOR Standard
From https://no-color.org/:
> Command-line software which adds ANSI color to its output by default should check for the presence of a NO_COLOR environment variable that, when present (regardless of its value), prevents the addition of ANSI color.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Colors appear in interactive terminal
- [ ] Colors disabled when piped
- [ ] Colors disabled when NO_COLOR is set
- [ ] Colors disabled in CI environments
- [ ] Gum only used in interactive TTY

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-19hm","title":"Implement per-machine baseline tests","description":"## Test Requirements: Per-Machine Baselines (Section 11.12)

### Unit Tests
1. **Baseline initialization**: Test initial baseline from first 24h of data
2. **Baseline update**: Test exponential moving average updates
3. **Baseline drift detection**: Test Wasserstein distance computation
4. **Baseline persistence**: Test save/load of baseline state

### Baseline Metrics Tests
```
METRICS:
- cpu_baseline: mean, std, p95 of per-process CPU
- memory_baseline: mean, std, p95 of per-process memory
- lifetime_baseline: mean, std of process lifetimes by type
- spawn_rate_baseline: processes spawned per hour
- kill_rate_baseline: processes killed per hour
- orphan_rate_baseline: orphans detected per hour
```

### Test Scenarios
```
SCENARIO: workload_shift
  Baseline: 50 processes, 10% CPU average
  Current: 100 processes, 20% CPU average
  Expected: Detect 2σ deviation, flag for attention

SCENARIO: gradual_drift
  Baseline: [50, 52, 54, 56, 58] processes over 5 days
  Expected: Detect upward drift, update baseline

SCENARIO: seasonal_pattern
  Baseline: Low activity weekends, high weekdays
  Current: Saturday with high activity
  Expected: Flag as anomaly

SCENARIO: hardware_change
  Baseline: 64 cores, 500GB RAM
  Current: 128 cores, 1TB RAM
  Expected: Reset baseline, use relative metrics
```

### Integration Tests
1. **Multi-day baseline**: Test baseline stability over 7 days
2. **Cross-reboot persistence**: Test baseline survives reboot
3. **Baseline reset**: Test manual baseline reset command
4. **Baseline export/import**: Test baseline transfer between machines

### Mathematical Tests
1. **EMA convergence**: Verify exponential moving average formula
2. **Wasserstein computation**: Verify W_1 distance formula
3. **Z-score accuracy**: Verify z = (x - μ) / σ

### Logging Requirements
- Log baseline updates with old/new values
- Log drift detections with distances
- Log anomaly detections with scores
- Log baseline reset events

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.

 {"id":"process_triage-1jn4","title":"Implement POMDP formulation for sequential process triage","description":"## Section 4.31 - POMDP Formulation

**Purpose**: Model process triage as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process. We can't directly see if a process is zombie—only noisy observations. Actions (probe, wait, kill) affect future states and observations.

**Mathematical Background**:
- POMDP tuple: (S, A, O, T, R, Ω, γ) - states, actions, observations, transitions, rewards, observation model, discount
- Belief MDP: Convert to fully observable over belief space b(s)
- Belief update: b'(s') ∝ Ω(o|s',a) Σ_s T(s'|s,a) b(s)
- Value iteration: V(b) = max_a [R(b,a) + γ Σ_o P(o|b,a) V(b')]
- PBVI: Point-based value iteration - sample beliefs, compute α-vectors
- POMCP: Monte Carlo tree search for online POMDP solving

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `define_triage_pomdp()` - States: {useful, bad, abandoned, zombie} × features
2. `belief_update(belief, action, observation)` - Bayes rule update
3. `pbvi_solver(pomdp, belief_points, iterations)` - Offline policy
4. `pomcp_action(pomdp, belief, simulations)` - Online action selection

**Why This Matters for pt**:
Full sequential optimization: 'Given current belief and remaining budget, should I probe more or kill now?' POMDP gives provably optimal policy (given the model).

**Integration Points**:
- Decision making (Section 5)
- Active sensing (Section 5.4)
- Deep scan orchestration (Section 3.3)

**Test Requirements**:
- Verify belief updates are correct via forward simulation
- Verify PBVI converges on small instances
- Compare POMCP policy to myopic heuristics","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead.  {"id":"process_triage-1k6","title":"Implement notification escalation ladder","description":"## Overview
Build the notification escalation system for dormant mode alerts.

## Background
The plan specifies an escalation ladder: desktop notification → email → SMS/PagerDuty. Severity increases based on confidence, resource impact, and how long findings have been ignored.

## Why It Matters
Not all findings are equally urgent. A single low-confidence abandoned process warrants a gentle notification. A high-confidence zombie consuming gigabytes of RAM needs immediate attention. Escalation ensures appropriate response.

## Technical Approach
1. Define escalation rules (triggers, timing, channels)
2. Implement notification providers (desktop, email, SMS)
3. Track notification state (sent, acknowledged, escalated)
4. Implement deduplication to avoid spam
5. Build digest aggregation for batch notifications

## Escalation Triggers
- Level 1 (Desktop): First detection, any confidence
- Level 2 (Email): Still present after 1 hour, confidence > 0.7
- Level 3 (SMS): Still present after 24 hours, confidence > 0.9
- Level 4 (Auto): If policy allows, auto-kill after 48 hours at > 0.95

## Notification Providers
- Desktop: notify-send (Linux), osascript (macOS), toast (Windows)
- Email: SMTP direct or via SendGrid/SES API
- SMS: Twilio API
- PagerDuty: Events API v2
- Webhook: Generic HTTP POST for custom integrations

## Deduplication Logic
- Track notification hash (process identity + finding type)
- Cooldown period before re-notifying for same issue
- Aggregate multiple findings into single notification
- Daily digest option instead of immediate notifications

## Acknowledgment Tracking
- Desktop: No ack tracking (fire and forget)
- Email: Read receipt or click tracking (optional)
- SMS: Reply-based acknowledgment
- PagerDuty: Native acknowledgment tracking

## Success Criteria
- All escalation levels functional
- Notifications delivered reliably
- No notification spam
- Acknowledgments tracked correctly

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-835.6","title":"Docs: Tutorials + examples (validated playbooks and scripts)","description":"## Purpose
Create **hands-on tutorials and examples** that make pt feel like a guided “alien artifact” instead of a pile of flags.

These tutorials should be designed to:
- build trust (show conservative behavior)
- teach evidence/ledger interpretation
- demonstrate safe automation patterns

## Scope
### Tutorials (minimum set)
1. First run: `pt scan` → interpret categories → run interactive `pt` safely.
2. Stuck test runner: identify, verify, staged terminate.
3. Port conflict: identify dev server, verify ownership, stop safely.
4. Agent workflow: session snapshot → plan/explain → apply → verify/diff.
5. Fleet workflow: multi-host plan and safe apply (plan-only in tutorial if destructive).

### Examples
- Example configs (`priors.json`, `policy.json`, redaction policy profiles).
- Example signature file snippets.
- Example `.ptb` bundle export and report generation.

## Deliverables
- `docs/tutorials/` markdown files and supporting sample artifacts.
- A small set of example scripts under `examples/` (non-destructive by default).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tutorials match the current CLI surface and output schemas.
- [ ] Examples are copy/paste runnable in “safe modes” (scan/plan-only) without requiring privileged operations.

## Test Plan
- E2E “tutorial runner”:
  - Execute example scripts in CI in scan/plan-only modes.
  - Validate emitted JSON examples against schemas.
- Logging:
  - On failure, log the tutorial step, command, and captured output.

 {"id":"process_triage-86l0","title":"Implement Bayesian inference correctness tests","description":"## Testing: Bayesian Inference Correctness

**Purpose**: Verify all Bayesian computations (posteriors, Bayes factors, credible intervals) are mathematically correct. Wrong inference = wrong decisions.

**Test Categories**:

1. **Conjugate Posterior Tests**:
```rust
#[test]
fn beta_binomial_posterior() {
    let prior = Beta::new(1.0, 1.0); // Uniform
    let data = vec![true, true, false, true]; // 3/4 successes
    let posterior = update_beta(prior, &data);
    
    // Posterior should be Beta(4, 2)
    assert_approx_eq!(posterior.alpha, 4.0);
    assert_approx_eq!(posterior.beta, 2.0);
    assert_approx_eq!(posterior.mean(), 0.667, 0.001);
}

#[test]
fn dirichlet_multinomial_posterior() {
    let prior = Dirichlet::new(vec![1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0]); // Uniform over 4 classes
    let counts = vec![10, 5, 3, 2]; // Observations
    let posterior = update_dirichlet(prior, &counts);
    
    // Posterior should be Dir(11, 6, 4, 3)
    assert_eq!(posterior.alpha, vec![11.0, 6.0, 4.0, 3.0]);
}
```

2. **Bayes Factor Tests**:
   - BF > 1 when data favors H1
   - BF = 1 for uninformative data
   - BF matches analytical formulas for known cases

3. **SPRT Tests**:
   - Stopping boundaries correctly computed
   - Average sample number matches theory
   - Type I/II error rates as specified

4. **Numerical Stability Tests**:
   - Log-space computations don't overflow
   - Near-zero probabilities handled
   - Very large datasets don't lose precision

**Logging Requirements**:
- Log intermediate computation values
- Log numerical precision warnings
- Log cases near stability boundaries

**Why This Matters**:
If Beta-Binomial update is wrong, all classification is wrong. Math tests are foundational.

**Test Fixtures**:
- Known posterior distributions (from statistical software)
- Extreme values for numerical stability
- Reference implementations for comparison","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead.  {"id":"process_triage-8ba","title":"Implement agent session management","description":"## Overview
Implement session management for multi-step agent workflows.

## Background
AI agents often need to perform multi-step workflows: scan, analyze, plan, verify, execute. Session management provides a context that persists across these steps, enabling stateful interactions.

## Why It Matters
Without sessions, each pt command is independent. Agents must pass full context every time. Sessions enable: transaction-like semantics, state persistence, and audit trails for multi-command workflows.

## Technical Approach
1. Session creation with unique ID
2. Session state storage (in-memory + optional persistence)
3. Session context passed via --session flag
4. Session timeout and cleanup
5. Session audit logging

## Session Lifecycle
1. pt session start [--ttl=3600] → Returns session_id
2. pt scan --session=XYZ → Scan attached to session
3. pt plan --session=XYZ → Plan references session scan
4. pt verify --session=XYZ → Verify plan still valid
5. pt execute --session=XYZ → Execute within session
6. pt session end --session=XYZ → Cleanup and final report

## Session State
- session_id: UUID
- created_at: Timestamp
- ttl_seconds: Time to live
- scan_result: Last scan in this session
- action_plan: Current plan (if any)
- execution_log: Actions taken in this session
- agent_metadata: Agent identity and context

## Session Commands
- session start: Create new session
- session status: Check session state
- session extend: Extend TTL
- session end: Cleanup and report
- session list: Show active sessions (admin)

## Persistence Options
- Memory-only (default): Fast, no disk IO
- File-based: Survives pt restart
- External store: Redis/SQLite for fleet coordination

## Success Criteria
- Sessions work across multiple pt invocations
- State persists correctly within TTL
- Session cleanup automatic
- Audit trail complete

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-8gfb","title":"Implement galaxy-brain mode","description":"## Task: Galaxy-Brain Mode (Phase 7.2)

### Description
Implement full mathematical transparency mode showing all equations, priors, likelihood ratios, and posterior computations.

### Requirements
1. **Mathematical Display**
   - Show actual formulas being computed
   - Display prior distributions with parameters
   - Show likelihood ratio calculations
   - Display posterior updates step-by-step
   - Show confidence interval derivations

2. **Evidence Equations**
   ```
   Prior: Beta(α=2, β=5) → P(abandoned) = 0.286
   
   Evidence 1: Age = 11 days
     Likelihood ratio: P(age|abandoned) / P(age|useful) = 45.3
     Posterior: Beta(α=2+1, β=5) → P(abandoned) = 0.375
   
   Evidence 2: CPU = 0% for 4 hours
     Likelihood ratio: P(cpu=0|abandoned) / P(cpu=0|useful) = 12.7
     Posterior update: P(abandoned) = 0.847
   
   Final: P(abandoned | all evidence) = 0.923
   Expected loss: kill=0.077×10 + spare=0.923×50 = 47.4
   Decision: KILL (minimize expected loss)
   ```

3. **Visualization**
   - Beta distribution plots (ASCII)
   - Likelihood ratio bars
   - Posterior evolution timeline
   - Decision boundary visualization

### Implementation Details
- Toggle with [g] key or --galaxy-brain flag
- Scrollable math panel in detail view
- Export to LaTeX for documentation
- Collapsible sections for each evidence type

### Mathematical Formulas to Display
1. Bayes' theorem: P(H|E) = P(E|H)P(H) / P(E)
2. Beta posterior: Beta(α + successes, β + failures)
3. Expected loss: Σ P(state) × L(action, state)
4. Confidence: 1 - P(error) for optimal decision
5. FDR control: q = Σe_i / n

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All mathematical formulas render correctly in terminal
- [ ] Equations match actual computations (validated)
- [ ] ASCII plots are legible at 80+ columns
- [ ] Mode toggles smoothly without UI flicker","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:03:03.283716317-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T04:03:03.283716317-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-8gfb","depends_on_id":"process_triage-2ka","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T06:49:57.569959096-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-8hb","title":"Implement dry-run mode","description":"## Overview
Implement dry-run mode that simulates all actions without executing them.

## Background
The plan specifies a --dry-run flag that runs the full pipeline—scan, score, recommend—but stops before executing any kills. This allows users to preview what would happen without risk.

## Why It Matters
Dry-run mode enables:
1. Testing: Verify configuration without consequences
2. Learning: See what pt would do in your environment
3. Auditing: Generate reports for review before approval
4. Scripting: Check candidates programmatically without action

## Technical Approach
1. Add --dry-run flag to CLI
2. Execute full pipeline up to action phase
3. Generate action plan but don't execute
4. Output what would have happened
5. Log dry-run as distinct event type

## Dry-Run Output
- Full candidate list with scores
- Recommended actions for each
- Expected outcomes (success probability)
- Policy checks that would apply
- Any warnings or blockers

## Output Formats
- Interactive: Show in TUI with '[DRY-RUN]' banner
- JSON: Full action plan for programmatic use
- Summary: Count and top candidates only

## Behavioral Guarantees
- No signals sent to any process
- No state modified (except log entry for dry-run)
- Read-only /proc access only
- No network calls (except telemetry if enabled)

## Success Criteria
- --dry-run produces identical recommendations to real run
- No side effects from dry-run
- Output clearly marked as dry-run
- Supports all output formats

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-8n3","title":"Define redaction and hashing policy for sensitive data","description":"## Purpose
Define the **redaction + hashing policy** that governs what `pt-core` is allowed to persist or display when process data contains sensitive information.

This bead is the policy contract. The implementation bead is `process_triage-k4yc.1`.

## Why This Matters (User Trust + Shareability)
Process triage inherently touches sensitive surfaces:
- command lines can include API keys/tokens/passwords
- environment variables often include secrets
- absolute paths leak usernames and project names
- hostnames/IPs leak infrastructure

The plan’s telemetry/bundle/report goals only work if users can safely share artifacts without fear of secret leakage.

## Threat Model (practical)
We assume attackers may obtain:
- session artifacts on disk
- `.ptb` bundles sent to others
- HTML reports emailed/slacked around

Therefore, “hash” must be resistant to trivial reversal/dictionary attacks. **Non-cryptographic hashes (e.g., FNV) are not acceptable for secrets.**

## Policy Concepts
### 1) Field Classes
Classify every string-like datum into a field class with default handling:
- `cmdline` (highest risk)
- `env_var_name` (low) / `env_var_value` (highest)
- `path`
- `hostname`
- `ip`
- `url`
- `username`
- `repo_identifier`
- `free_text` (logs/messages)

### 2) Actions
Each rule produces one of:
- `allow` (persist as-is)
- `redact` (remove or replace with `[REDACTED]`)
- `hash` (replace with stable keyed hash)
- `normalize` (lossy pattern replacement, possibly followed by hash)

### 3) Profiles (export/report intent)
Profiles constrain what can be included and how aggressively it is sanitized:
- `minimal`: only high-level plan/summary/aggregate stats; no raw strings
- `safe`: includes evidence ledger + derived features, but raw strings are redacted/hashed/normalized
- `forensic`: includes more raw evidence **only if explicitly allowed by policy**, and may require encryption

Profiles must be deterministic and recorded in artifacts (session + bundle + report).

## Hashing Policy (normative)
### Keyed hashing (required)
- Use a **keyed cryptographic hash** for `hash` rules: e.g. `HMAC-SHA256(key, canonical_string)`.
- Output format: `hmac256:<salt_id>:<truncated_hex>` (truncate length is a policy parameter).

Rationale:
- Stable within a key/salt_id so patterns can match across sessions.
- Resistant to trivial reversal compared to unkeyed hashes.

### Key storage
- Store the redaction key/salt in the config dir with strict permissions (e.g., `0600`).
- Key must never be written to telemetry/bundles/reports.

### Canonicalization before hashing
To keep hashes useful for pattern matching (without leaking details):
- trim whitespace
- collapse repeated whitespace
- normalize paths (e.g., home dir → `[HOME]`, temp dirs → `[TMP]`)
- normalize digits/ports/PIDs where appropriate
- normalize URL credentials (`user:pass@` → `***@`)

Canonicalization must be versioned because it changes hash outputs.

## Redaction/Normalization Rules (examples)
- `API_KEY=sk-...` → `API_KEY=[REDACTED]`
- `/home/alice/project-x/src` → `[HOME]/[HASH:<...>]/src` (policy decides which segments are hashed)
- `--token=abc123` → `--token=[REDACTED]`
- `https://user:pass@host/path` → `https://[REDACTED]@host/path` (then optionally hash host)

## Governance & Auditability Requirements
Every session/bundle/report must record:
- `redaction_policy_version`
- `redaction_key_id` (not the key)
- canonicalization version
- profile used

So a reviewer can understand *what guarantees apply*.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Policy explicitly defines: field classes, actions, hashing algorithm requirements, canonicalization rules, and export profiles.
- [ ] Hashing is keyed cryptographic (no unkeyed/non-crypto hashes for secrets).
- [ ] Policy is versioned and the version is recorded in all persisted artifacts.
- [ ] The policy is precise enough that the implementation (`process_triage-k4yc.1`) can be written without consulting the plan doc.

## Test Plan
- Unit (policy parsing):
  - reject invalid configs
  - ensure required keys exist
- Golden redaction tests:
  - a fixed set of “canary strings” representing secrets/paths/usernames must never appear in persisted outputs when policy says redact/hash
- Hashing tests:
  - stability under same key_id
  - change under key rotation
- Logging tests:
  - logs must not leak raw secrets even when errors occur

 {"id":"process_triage-rqef","title":"Implement PAC-Bayes generalization bounds","description":"## Section 4.33 - PAC-Bayes Generalization

**Purpose**: Provide probably approximately correct (PAC) bounds on generalization error of Bayesian classifiers. Ensure learned priors generalize to new processes, not just training data.

**Mathematical Background**:
- PAC-Bayes bound: E_ρ[L(h)] ≤ E_ρ[L̂(h)] + √[(KL(ρ||π) + log(2√n/δ))/(2n)]
  where ρ is posterior, π is prior, L̂ is empirical loss
- Data-dependent prior: π can depend on part of data (split trick)
- McAllester's bound: Uses KL directly, tightest for small KL
- Catoni's bound: PAC-Bayes with tighter constants
- Localized bounds: Condition on ρ being close to π

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `pac_bayes_bound(posterior, prior, empirical_risk, n, delta)` - Compute bound
2. `optimal_posterior(prior, data, regularization)` - Minimize PAC-Bayes bound
3. `generalization_gap(training_error, pac_bound)` - Expected test error
4. `data_split_prior(data, split_ratio)` - Use part of data for prior

**Why This Matters for pt**:
Our classifier learns from user decisions. PAC-Bayes guarantees: 'With 95% probability, error rate on new processes ≤ 0.15'. This is stronger than just training accuracy.

**Integration Points**:
- Model validation (Section 4.14)
- Decision learning (Section 5.8)
- Confidence reporting (Section 7.1)

**Test Requirements**:
- Verify bound holds on held-out data

 {"id":"process_triage-rqn","title":"Implement Beta distribution utilities","description":"## Task
Implement Beta distribution functions needed for posterior computation.

## Background
The Beta distribution is conjugate to Bernoulli/Binomial and appears throughout:
- Prior on Bernoulli success probability
- Posterior after observing successes/failures
- Posterior predictive for next observation

Required functions:
- beta_pdf(x, alpha, beta) - probability density
- beta_cdf(x, alpha, beta) - cumulative distribution
- beta_inv_cdf(p, alpha, beta) - quantile function
- beta_mean(alpha, beta) = alpha / (alpha + beta)
- beta_var(alpha, beta) = alpha*beta / ((alpha+beta)^2 * (alpha+beta+1))
- log_beta_pdf(x, alpha, beta) - log-domain for stability

## Implementation Notes
- Use log-beta function for numerical stability
- log_beta(a,b) = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a+b)
- Handle edge cases: x=0, x=1, alpha<1, beta<1
- Consider using Stirling approximation for large parameters

## Test Cases
- Beta(1,1) = Uniform(0,1)
- Beta(2,5): mean=2/7, mode=1/5
- Symmetry: Beta(a,b) at x = Beta(b,a) at 1-x
- Known quantiles from tables

## Deliverables
- Rust module: math/beta.rs
- Unit tests with comprehensive coverage
- Documentation with equations
- Benchmarks for performance verification

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `beta_mean`/`beta_var` match analytic values for common parameters.
- [ ] `beta_pdf` and `log_beta_pdf` agree (exp(log_pdf) ~= pdf) where numerically safe.
- [ ] `beta_cdf` is monotone and returns 0 at x=0 and 1 at x=1 (within tolerance).
- [ ] `beta_inv_cdf` inverts `beta_cdf` on a representative grid of p values.
- [ ] Edge cases (alpha/beta < 1, extreme alpha/beta) do not produce NaN/Inf for valid inputs.

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden values (mean/var/mode), symmetry checks, boundary x∈{0,1}.
- Property: inversion (`cdf(inv_cdf(p))≈p`), monotonicity.
- Perf: micro-benchmarks for cdf/inv_cdf to ensure feasibility at scale.

-{"id":"process_triage-rtb","title":"Implement failure recovery and retry logic","description":"## Overview
Implement robust failure recovery and intelligent retry logic for action execution.

## Background
Process termination can fail for many reasons: permission denied, process already gone, signal handling by the process, race conditions. The plan specifies a staged approach: SIGTERM → wait → SIGKILL, with exponential backoff and configurable retries.

## Why It Matters
Naive retry logic can cause confusion (multiple signals to same PID), waste time (retrying permission errors), or miss opportunities (process respawned with new PID). Intelligent failure handling improves reliability and user experience.

## Technical Approach
1. Classify failure types:
   - Transient (EAGAIN, timeout) → retry with backoff
   - Permanent (EPERM, ESRCH) → fail fast with clear message
   - Partial (SIGTERM ignored) → escalate to SIGKILL
2. Implement exponential backoff with jitter
3. Track attempt history for audit log
4. Detect respawned processes (same command, new PID)
5. Provide structured failure reports

## Signal Escalation Protocol
- Attempt 1: SIGTERM, wait policy.json:term_grace_seconds (default 5s)
- Attempt 2: If still running, SIGKILL
- Attempt 3: If still running after SIGKILL, report as unkillable (kernel zombie or protected)

## Success Criteria
- Transient failures auto-recovered
- Permanent failures reported immediately
- Signal escalation works correctly
- Respawned process detection functional
- Comprehensive failure audit trail

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-s8s","title":"EPIC: Phase 15 - Enhanced UX for Agents","description":"## Overview
Implement enhanced explainability features specifically designed for AI agent consumption. Includes genealogy narratives, blast radius analysis, what-if explanations, and human-friendly summary modes.

## Core Requirements (from Plan Sections 7.9-7.12)

### 7.9 Genealogy Narrative Generation
Build process ancestry chain with role annotations and generate human-readable story.

**Ancestry Chain Construction**:
- Walk PPID tree to root (init/systemd)
- Annotate each node with role: worker, test_runner, user_shell, daemon, container_shim, etc.
- Detect common patterns: 'spawn from Claude Code session', 'fork from build system'

**Narrative Generation**:
- Convert structured ancestry to prose: 'This process was spawned by a Jest test runner, which was started from a Claude Code session initiated by user ubuntu'
- Include temporal context: 'started 3 hours ago during what appears to be a testing session'

**Output Format**:
- `pt agent explain --genealogy` produces structured JSON and optional prose
- Include in plan output when process ancestry is relevant

### 7.10 Blast Radius Visualization
Analyze and quantify the impact of killing a process.

**Dependency Analysis**:
- Children enumeration (direct and transitive)
- Port dependents: other processes connecting to ports this process listens on
- File dependents: processes with open handles to same files
- Shared memory segments
- IPC connections (Unix sockets, pipes)

**Cumulative Risk Score**:
- Weighted sum of: child count, dependent count, connection count, open files
- Categorize as: LOW (contained), MEDIUM (some impact), HIGH (significant cascade)

**Output**:
```json
{
  'blast_radius': {
    'memory_mb': 1200,
    'cpu_pct': 98,
    'child_count': 3,
    'connection_count': 2,
    'open_files': 47,
    'dependent_processes': [...],
    'risk_level': 'low',
    'summary': 'Killing this process will free 1.2GB RAM...'
  }
}
```

### 7.11 What Would Change Your Mind
Identify flip conditions for each recommendation.

**Flip Condition Identification**:
- For each feature, compute delta_p: how much would posterior change if this evidence changed?
- Identify most impactful evidence that could reverse recommendation

**Examples**:
- 'If this process showed any IO activity in the last 30 minutes, P(abandoned) would drop to 0.3'
- 'If the controlling TTY were still active, this would likely be classified as useful'

**Output Format**:
- `pt agent explain --what-if` produces flip conditions
- Rank by impact and feasibility of verification

### 7.12 Human-Friendly Summary Modes
Different output formats for different consumption contexts.

**--brief Mode**:
- Minimal output for quick scanning
- One line per candidate: 'PID 1234 [KILL] jest worker - abandoned (conf: 0.94)'

**--narrative Mode**:
- Prose explanations suitable for reports and logs
- 'Process 1234 appears to be an abandoned Jest test worker that has been idle for 3 hours...'

**Structured Summary** (default for pt agent plan):
- Top-line summary: 'Found 5 candidates: 2 KILL, 2 REVIEW, 1 SPARE'
- Action breakdown with confidence
- Resource recovery potential
- Risk assessment

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Genealogy narrative generation works
- [ ] `--genealogy` flag produces structured + prose output
- [ ] Blast radius analysis implemented
- [ ] Risk level categorization works
- [ ] What-if flip condition identification implemented
- [ ] `--what-if` produces ranked flip conditions
- [ ] `--brief` mode produces minimal output
- [ ] `--narrative` mode produces prose explanations
- [ ] Default structured summary is informative

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Phase 4 (inference), Phase 6 (action execution), Process tree analysis
- Blocks: None (enhancement)

## Technical Notes
- Genealogy walks should be cached per scan
- Blast radius analysis uses /proc/<pid>/fd and lsof
- What-if uses gradient/sensitivity analysis on posterior","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T03:52:27.506681081-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T03:52:27.506681081-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-s8s","depends_on_id":"process_triage-h89","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:05:19.338771644-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-s8s","depends_on_id":"process_triage-sj6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:09:17.317432367-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-sj6","title":"EPIC: Phase 6 - Action Execution System","description":"## Overview
This epic covers the action execution system that safely applies decisions to processes.

## Background & Context
Section 6 specifies an action space beyond just kill:
- keep, pause, resume, renice, cgroup freeze, cgroup throttle
- cpuset quarantine, supervisor stop/restart
- zombie resolution (parent reaping)
- kill (SIGTERM → SIGKILL)

Actions must be:
- Safe (identity verified, privilege checked)
- Staged (less destructive first)
- Reversible (when possible)
- Auditable (fully logged)

## Why This Matters
- **Safety**: Wrong action on wrong process is catastrophic
- **Reversibility**: Prefer pause over kill when possible
- **Coordination**: Handle supervised processes correctly
- **Auditability**: Every action must be traceable

## Scope
1. Action plan generation (per-PID with ordering)
2. Identity revalidation before action
3. Staged execution protocol
4. Post-action verification
5. Failure recovery trees
6. Session/TTY protection
7. Lock coordination

## Success Criteria
- No PID reuse errors in testing
- Actions respect staging order
- Failures trigger recovery tree
- All actions logged with outcomes

## Technical Constraints
- Must revalidate (pid, start_id, uid) before action
- Default to same-UID only
- Acquire pt lock before action
- Protect current session

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A plan can be executed end-to-end with staged steps and post-action verification.
- [ ] Identity revalidation blocks stale/unsafe actions (PID reuse) deterministically.
- [ ] Zombie and D-state processes are handled via safe alternatives (no blind kill).
- [ ] Failure recovery trees are attached to actions and drive retries/fallbacks.
- [ ] All actions produce auditable outcome records (telemetry + human summary).

## Test Plan
- Unit: plan generation rules, identity checks, recovery tree selection.
- Integration: apply actions to controlled fixture processes (term/kill/respawn).
- E2E: golden-path workflow including after-action summary.

+//! Galaxy-brain mode math transparency types.
+//!
+//! This module defines types for the "galaxy-brain" mode that exposes
+//! full mathematical reasoning of the inference engine.
+
+use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
+use std::collections::HashMap;
+
+/// Schema version for galaxy-brain data.
+pub const GALAXY_BRAIN_SCHEMA_VERSION: &str = "1.0.0";
+
+/// Stable card identifiers for programmatic access.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]
+#[serde(rename_all = "snake_case")]
+pub enum CardId {
+    /// Posterior class probability breakdown.
+    PosteriorCore,
+    /// Time-varying hazard rates with Gamma posteriors.
+    HazardTimeVarying,
+    /// Conformal prediction intervals for runtime/CPU.
+    ConformalInterval,
+    /// Conformal classification prediction sets.
+    ConformalClassSet,
+    /// E-values and anytime-valid FDR control.
+    EValuesFdr,
+    /// Alpha-investing online testing budget.
+    AlphaInvesting,
+    /// Value of information for next probe.
+    Voi,
+}
+
+impl CardId {
+    /// Get all card IDs in display order.
+    pub fn all() -> &'static [CardId] {
+        &[
+            CardId::PosteriorCore,
+            CardId::HazardTimeVarying,
+            CardId::ConformalInterval,
+            CardId::ConformalClassSet,
+            CardId::EValuesFdr,
+            CardId::AlphaInvesting,
+            CardId::Voi,
+        ]
+    }
+
+    /// Get the default title for this card.
+    pub fn default_title(&self) -> &'static str {
+        match self {
+            CardId::PosteriorCore => "Posterior Class Probabilities",
+            CardId::HazardTimeVarying => "Time-Varying Hazard Rates",
+            CardId::ConformalInterval => "Conformal Prediction Intervals",
+            CardId::ConformalClassSet => "Conformal Classification Set",
+            CardId::EValuesFdr => "E-values and Anytime-Valid FDR",
+            CardId::AlphaInvesting => "Alpha-Investing Budget State",
+            CardId::Voi => "Value of Information",
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Get the index in display order.
+    pub fn index(&self) -> usize {
+        Self::all().iter().position(|c| c == self).unwrap_or(0)
+    }
+}
+
+/// Full galaxy-brain data for a process.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
+pub struct GalaxyBrainData {
+    /// Schema version.
+    pub schema_version: String,
+
+    /// Process ID this data applies to.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub process_id: Option<i32>,
+
+    /// Session ID for correlation.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub session_id: Option<String>,
+
+    /// When this snapshot was generated.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub generated_at: Option<String>,
+
+    /// Math transparency cards.
+    pub cards: Vec<MathCard>,
+
+    /// Rendering hints.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub render_hints: Option<RenderHints>,
+}
+
+impl Default for GalaxyBrainData {
+    fn default() -> Self {
+        Self {
+            schema_version: GALAXY_BRAIN_SCHEMA_VERSION.to_string(),
+            process_id: None,
+            session_id: None,
+            generated_at: None,
+            cards: Vec::new(),
+            render_hints: None,
+        }
+    }
+}
+
+/// A single math transparency card.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
+pub struct MathCard {
+    /// Stable card identifier.
+    pub id: CardId,
+
+    /// Human-readable title.
+    pub title: String,
+
+    /// Optional subtitle.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub subtitle: Option<String>,
+
+    /// LaTeX equations showing the math.
+    pub equations: Vec<Equation>,
+
+    /// Concrete computed values.
+    pub values: HashMap<String, ComputedValue>,
+
+    /// One-line plain-English explanation.
+    pub intuition: String,
+
+    /// Optional longer explanation.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub details: Option<String>,
+
+    /// Optional warnings about computation.
+    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]
+    pub warnings: Vec<String>,
+
+    /// Optional academic references.
+    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]
+    pub references: Vec<Reference>,
+
+    /// Whether collapsed by default in TUI.
+    #[serde(default)]
+    pub collapsed_by_default: bool,
+}
+
+impl MathCard {
+    /// Create a new card with the given ID.
+    pub fn new(id: CardId) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            id,
+            title: id.default_title().to_string(),
+            subtitle: None,
+            equations: Vec::new(),
+            values: HashMap::new(),
+            intuition: String::new(),
+            details: None,
+            warnings: Vec::new(),
+            references: Vec::new(),
+            collapsed_by_default: false,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Add an equation to the card.
+    pub fn with_equation(mut self, equation: Equation) -> Self {
+        self.equations.push(equation);
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Add a computed value to the card.
+    pub fn with_value(mut self, key: impl Into<String>, value: ComputedValue) -> Self {
+        self.values.insert(key.into(), value);
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Set the intuition string.
+    pub fn with_intuition(mut self, intuition: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        self.intuition = intuition.into();
+        self
+    }
+}
+
+/// A mathematical equation with rendering information.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
+pub struct Equation {
+    /// LaTeX/KaTeX source.
+    pub latex: String,
+
+    /// True for block display, false for inline.
+    #[serde(default = "default_true")]
+    pub display_mode: bool,
+
+    /// Optional label (e.g., "Bayes rule").
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub label: Option<String>,
+
+    /// ASCII fallback for terminals without math rendering.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub ascii_fallback: Option<String>,
+}
+
+impl Equation {
+    /// Create a new display equation.
+    pub fn display(latex: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            latex: latex.into(),
+            display_mode: true,
+            label: None,
+            ascii_fallback: None,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Create a new inline equation.
+    pub fn inline(latex: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            latex: latex.into(),
+            display_mode: false,
+            label: None,
+            ascii_fallback: None,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Add a label.
+    pub fn with_label(mut self, label: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        self.label = Some(label.into());
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Add an ASCII fallback.
+    pub fn with_ascii(mut self, ascii: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        self.ascii_fallback = Some(ascii.into());
+        self
+    }
+}
+
+/// A concrete computed numeric value.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
+pub struct ComputedValue {
+    /// The computed value.
+    pub value: ValueType,
+
+    /// Unit of measurement.
+    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
+    pub unit: Option<String>,
+
+    /// Display format.

+# Galaxy-Brain Mode: Math Card Specifications
+
+This document defines the 7 math transparency cards exposed in galaxy-brain mode. Each card shows a specific aspect of the Bayesian inference engine.
+
+## Overview
+
+Galaxy-brain mode (activated via `g` keybinding in TUI or `--galaxy-brain` flag) exposes the full mathematical reasoning behind process classification decisions. This serves dual purposes:
+
+1. **Educational**: Understanding the "alien artifact" math
+2. **Debugging**: Verifying inference is behaving correctly
+
+## Card Schema
+
+Each card has the following structure:
+
+```json
+{
+  "id": "card_identifier",
+  "title": "Human-Readable Title",
+  "equations": [{"latex": "...", "ascii_fallback": "..."}],
+  "values": {"key": {"value": 0.95, "label": "...", "symbol": "..."}},
+  "intuition": "One-line plain-English explanation"
+}
+```
+
+See `specs/schemas/galaxy-brain.schema.json` for the full JSON Schema.
+
+---
+
+## Card 1: `posterior_core`
+
+**Title**: Posterior Class Probabilities
+
+**Purpose**: Shows the full Bayesian posterior breakdown: log P(C|x) with all terms.
+
+### Equations
+
+```latex
+\log P(C|x) = \log P(x|C) + \log P(C) - \log P(x)
+```
+
+```latex
+P(C|x) = \frac{P(x|C) \cdot P(C)}{\sum_{c'} P(x|c') \cdot P(c')}
+```
+
+### Values
+
+| Key | Symbol | Description |
+|-----|--------|-------------|
+| `log_likelihood_useful` | `log P(x|useful)` | Log-likelihood under useful class |
+| `log_likelihood_abandoned` | `log P(x|abandoned)` | Log-likelihood under abandoned class |
+| `log_likelihood_zombie` | `log P(x|zombie)` | Log-likelihood under zombie class |
+| `log_prior_useful` | `log P(useful)` | Log prior probability of useful |
+| `log_prior_abandoned` | `log P(abandoned)` | Log prior for abandoned |
+| `log_prior_zombie` | `log P(zombie)` | Log prior for zombie |
+| `log_evidence` | `log P(x)` | Log marginal likelihood (normalizer) |
+| `posterior_useful` | `P(useful|x)` | Final posterior probability |
+| `posterior_abandoned` | `P(abandoned|x)` | Final posterior probability |
+| `posterior_zombie` | `P(zombie|x)` | Final posterior probability |
+| `max_class` | - | Highest probability class |
+
+### Intuition
+
+> "Given this process's evidence, there's a {posterior_abandoned}% chance it's abandoned."
+
+---
+
+## Card 2: `hazard_time_varying`
+
+**Title**: Time-Varying Hazard Rates
+
+**Purpose**: Shows regime-specific hazard rates with Gamma posteriors, modeling how the probability of abandonment changes over time.
+
+### Equations
+
+```latex
+h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)} = \text{instantaneous failure rate}
+```
+
+```latex
+h(t) \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha_t, \beta_t) \quad \text{(posterior)}
+```
+
+```latex
+E[h(t)] = \frac{\alpha_t}{\beta_t}, \quad \text{Var}[h(t)] = \frac{\alpha_t}{\beta_t^2}
+```
+
+### Values
+
+| Key | Symbol | Description |
+|-----|--------|-------------|
+| `current_regime` | - | Current time regime (startup/active/aged) |
+| `regime_boundaries_sec` | - | Time thresholds between regimes |
+| `hazard_rate_startup` | `h_{startup}` | Hazard rate in startup regime |
+| `hazard_rate_active` | `h_{active}` | Hazard rate in active regime |
+| `hazard_rate_aged` | `h_{aged}` | Hazard rate in aged regime |
+| `gamma_alpha` | `α` | Gamma posterior shape parameter |
+| `gamma_beta` | `β` | Gamma posterior rate parameter |
+| `survival_prob` | `S(t)` | Current survival probability |
+
+### Intuition
+
+> "Process has been running {runtime}, now in '{current_regime}' regime with hazard rate {hazard_rate}."
+
+---
+
+## Card 3: `conformal_interval`
+
+**Title**: Conformal Prediction Intervals
+
+**Purpose**: Shows prediction intervals for runtime and CPU usage using conformal prediction (distribution-free coverage guarantees).
+
+### Equations
+
+```latex
+\hat{C}_\alpha(x) = \{y : s(x, y) \leq Q_{1-\alpha}(\{s_i\}_{i=1}^n)\}
+```
+
+```latex
+P(Y_{n+1} \in \hat{C}_\alpha(X_{n+1})) \geq 1 - \alpha
+```
+
+### Values
+
+| Key | Symbol | Description |
+|-----|--------|-------------|
+| `runtime_interval_lower` | - | Lower bound of runtime interval (seconds) |
+| `runtime_interval_upper` | - | Upper bound of runtime interval (seconds) |
+| `runtime_interval_coverage` | `1-α` | Nominal coverage (e.g., 0.90) |
+| `cpu_interval_lower` | - | Lower bound of CPU usage interval |
+| `cpu_interval_upper` | - | Upper bound of CPU usage interval |
+| `conformity_score` | `s(x,y)` | Non-conformity score for this process |
+| `calibration_set_size` | `n` | Number of calibration examples |
+
+### Intuition
+
+> "With 90% confidence, this process will run between {runtime_lower} and {runtime_upper}."
+
+---
+
+## Card 4: `conformal_class_set`
+
+**Title**: Conformal Classification Set
+
+**Purpose**: Shows the prediction set for classification with calibrated p-values.
+
+### Equations
+
+```latex
+\hat{C}_\alpha(x) = \{c : p(c|x) \geq \alpha\}
+```
+
+```latex
+p(c|x) = \frac{|\{i : s(x_i, c) \geq s(x, c)\}| + 1}{n + 1}
+```
+
+### Values
+
+| Key | Symbol | Description |
+|-----|--------|-------------|
+| `prediction_set` | `Ĉ(x)` | Set of classes in prediction set |
+| `p_value_useful` | `p(useful|x)` | Conformal p-value for useful |
+| `p_value_abandoned` | `p(abandoned|x)` | Conformal p-value for abandoned |
+| `p_value_zombie` | `p(zombie|x)` | Conformal p-value for zombie |
+| `alpha_level` | `α` | Significance level |
+| `set_size` | - | Size of prediction set |
+
+### Intuition
+
+> "At α={alpha}, the prediction set is {prediction_set}. {set_size == 1 ? 'High confidence!' : 'Multiple plausible classes.'}"
+
+---
+
+## Card 5: `e_values_fdr`
+
+**Title**: E-values and Anytime-Valid FDR Control
+
+**Purpose**: Shows e-values for sequential testing and e-FDR control.
+
+### Equations
+
+```latex
+e(x) = \frac{P(x|H_1)}{P(x|H_0)} \quad \text{(likelihood ratio e-value)}
+```
+
+```latex
+\text{e-BH threshold}: e_i \geq \frac{n}{k \cdot \alpha} \quad \text{for } k \text{-th smallest}
+```
+
+```latex
+E[\#\text{false discoveries} / e_{\text{sum}}] \leq \alpha
+```
+
+### Values
+
+| Key | Symbol | Description |
+|-----|--------|-------------|
+| `e_value` | `e(x)` | E-value for this process |
+| `log_e_value` | `log e(x)` | Log e-value |
+| `e_fdr_threshold` | - | Current e-BH rejection threshold |
+| `rejected` | - | Whether this process is rejected (abandoned) |
+| `running_e_sum` | `Σe` | Running sum of e-values |
+| `false_discovery_bound` | - | Bound on expected false discoveries |
+
+### Intuition
+
+> "E-value of {e_value}: evidence against 'useful' hypothesis is {strong/weak}."
+
+---
+
+## Card 6: `alpha_investing`
+
+**Title**: Alpha-Investing Budget State
+
+**Purpose**: Shows the current state of the alpha-investing algorithm for online hypothesis testing.
+
+### Equations
+
+```latex
+W_t = W_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t-1} (\phi_i \cdot R_i - \alpha_i)
+```
+
+```latex
+\alpha_t = \min(\psi(W_t), \alpha_{\max})
+```
+
+```latex
+\text{mFDR} = E\left[\frac{V}{R \vee 1}\right] \leq \alpha
+```
+
+### Values
+
+| Key | Symbol | Description |
+|-----|--------|-------------|
+| `current_wealth` | `W_t` | Current wealth/budget |
+| `initial_wealth` | `W_0` | Initial wealth |
+| `alpha_spent` | `Σα` | Total alpha spent so far |
+| `rejections` | `R` | Number of rejections so far |
+| `current_alpha` | `α_t` | Alpha level for current test |
+| `wealth_earned` | - | Wealth earned from rejections |
+| `mfdr_guarantee` | - | Modified FDR guarantee |
+
+### Intuition
+
+> "Budget: {current_wealth}. Can test {n_remaining} more hypotheses before budget depletes."
+
+---
+
+## Card 7: `voi`
+
+**Title**: Value of Information
+
+**Purpose**: Shows which additional probes (deep scans, syscall traces) would provide the most information gain.
+
+### Equations
+
+```latex
+\text{VOI}(\text{probe}) = E[\text{max}_a U(a | x, \text{probe})] - \text{max}_a U(a | x)
+```
+
+```latex
+\text{VOI}(\text{probe}) \approx H(C|x) - E[H(C|x, \text{probe})]
+```
+
+### Values
+
+| Key | Symbol | Description |
+|-----|--------|-------------|
+| `current_entropy` | `H(C|x)` | Current posterior entropy (uncertainty) |
+| `voi_deep_scan` | - | Expected info gain from deep scan |
+| `voi_syscall_trace` | - | Expected info gain from syscall trace |
+| `voi_network_probe` | - | Expected info gain from network probe |
+| `recommended_probe` | - | Highest-VOI probe |
+| `cost_benefit` | - | VOI / cost ratio for each probe |
+
+### Intuition
+
+> "Current uncertainty: {entropy} bits. A {recommended_probe} would reduce uncertainty by ~{expected_reduction} bits."
+
+---
+
+## Rendering Specifications
+
+### TUI Rendering
+
+- Toggle with `g` keybinding
+- Use Unicode math symbols when terminal supports it: α, β, Σ, ∈
+- ASCII fallbacks: alpha, beta, sum, in
+- Color scheme: equations in cyan, values in yellow, intuition in green
+- Collapsible cards with expand/collapse toggle
+
+### CLI Rendering
+
+```
+$ pt show --galaxy-brain --pid 12345
+
+=== Galaxy-Brain: Mathematical Transparency ===
+
+[1/7] Posterior Class Probabilities
+  log P(useful|x)    = -2.34
+  log P(abandoned|x) = -0.89  ← MAX
+  log P(zombie|x)    = -4.12
+
+  Intuition: 58% chance this process is abandoned.
+
+[2/7] Time-Varying Hazard Rates
+  ...
+```
+
+### Report Tab
+
+- Dedicated "Galaxy Brain" tab in HTML reports
+- KaTeX rendering for equations
+- Interactive: click equation to see derivation
+- Expandable details for each card
+- Chart visualizations where applicable (hazard curves, intervals)
+
+---
+
+## Implementation Notes
+
+1. **Caching**: Galaxy-brain data can be regenerated from stored inference state
+2. **Performance**: Compute lazily on toggle, cache for responsive switching
+3. **Versioning**: Card IDs are stable; add new cards with new IDs
+4. **Localization**: Intuition strings should support i18n
